  2. Maps tokens to cost using provider pricing tables.
  3. Aggregates cost by plan stage, plugin, and model.
  4. Surfaces a user-facing cost report.

docs.title: Token Event Schema
docs.text:
{
  "plan_id": "plan_123",
  "stage": "assume",
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
  "input_tokens": 4200,
  "output_tokens": 900,
  "provider_cost_usd": 0.034
}

docs.title: Aggregation Schema
docs.text:
{
  "plan_id": "plan_123",
  "total_cost_usd": 1.42,
  "by_stage": {
    "assume": 0.35,
    "risk": 0.22,
    "finance": 0.47
  },
  "by_model": {
    "gpt-4o-mini": 0.78,
    "gemini-2.0-flash": 0.64
  }
}

docs.title: Reporting Views
docs.text:
• Plan Cost Summary: total tokens, total cost, top cost drivers.
  • Stage Breakdown: cost per pipeline stage.
  • Model Breakdown: cost per model/provider.
  • Optimization Insights: suggestions to reduce high-cost stages.

docs.title: Governance Features
docs.text:
• Cost caps per plan or per day.
  • Alerts when costs exceed thresholds.
  • Audit logs for cost anomalies.

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Works with all pipeline stages and plugins.
  • Feeds budgeting dashboards.
  • Used in governance and allocation decisions.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Cost visibility for 100% of plans.
  • Reduction in cost per plan after optimization.
  • Fewer cost overruns and unexpected bills.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Provider token counts may change or be inconsistent.
  • Cost reporting overhead adds latency.
  • Misinterpretation of cost data by users.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Per-user or per-team cost budgeting.
  • Predictive cost estimation before plan generation.
  • Multi-currency cost reporting.

docs.title: 1) Instrumentation in llm.py (source of truth)
docs.text:
Implement a provider-normalization layer around every outbound model call:

  1. Capture request metadata before call:
  2. run_id, stage, provider, model, prompt_variant, started_at
  3. Execute provider call unchanged.
  4. Read raw provider response and parse usage fields:
  5. OpenAI-style: usage.prompt_tokens, usage.completion_tokens, reasoning fields when present
  6. Anthropic-style: input_tokens, output_tokens, thinking-token fields when present
  7. Gemini/OpenRouter: normalized usage from provider envelope
  8. Persist raw usage payload JSON for audit (raw_usage_json) plus normalized fields.

docs.title: 2) Token schema and persistence
docs.text:
Create a DB table such as:

CREATE TABLE llm_call_usage (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  run_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  stage TEXT NOT NULL,
  provider TEXT NOT NULL,
  model TEXT NOT NULL,
  input_tokens INT,
  output_tokens INT,
  reasoning_tokens INT,
  cached_tokens INT,
  cost_usd NUMERIC(12,6),
  latency_ms INT,
  raw_usage_json JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

And a summary view/table:

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW llm_run_usage_summary AS
SELECT run_id,
       SUM(input_tokens) AS input_tokens,
       SUM(output_tokens) AS output_tokens,
       SUM(reasoning_tokens) AS reasoning_tokens,
       SUM(cost_usd) AS total_cost_usd,
       COUNT(*) AS call_count
FROM llm_call_usage
GROUP BY run_id;

docs.title: 3) Cost engine
docs.text:
Add a pricing_catalog keyed by provider+model with time-versioned rates: - input per 1k tokens - output per 1k tokens - reasoning per 1k tokens (if billed separately)

Cost formula per call:

cost = (input_tokens/1000)*rate_in + (output_tokens/1000)*rate_out + (reasoning_tokens/1000)*rate_reason

Store calculated cost and the pricing_version used for reproducibility.

docs.title: 4) API/report integration
docs.text:
• Extend run status endpoint with:
  • total tokens and cost
  • stage-by-stage usage table
  • model/provider breakdown
  • Add a report section in generated plan artifacts:
  • “Cost & Token Accounting”
  • includes confidence note when provider usage is partially missing.

docs.title: 5) Structured output handling rule
docs.text: Critical implementation detail: - Usage is captured from provider raw envelope before JSON parsing/validation. - Structured-output parse failures should not lose token accounting.

docs.title: 6) Reliability and edge cases
docs.text:
• If provider usage fields missing:
  • mark usage_quality = estimated
  • optional fallback tokenizer estimate
  • For retries:
  • log each retry as independent call record
  • include attempt_number
  • For streaming:
  • aggregate chunk usage if available; else finalize from closing usage frame.

docs.title: 7) Rollout phases
docs.text:
• Phase A: capture + store usage only (no UI)
  • Phase B: cost engine + summary endpoint
  • Phase C: user-visible report + budget alerts
  • Phase D: optimization recommendations (cost hot spots)

docs.title: 8) Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Unit tests for provider mapping parsers
  • Golden tests with canned raw provider responses
  • Billing reconciliation tests against provider invoices
  • Backfill script for historical runs where data exists

docs.title: Deployment Path
docs.text:
1. Ship instrumentation behind TOKEN_ACCOUNTING_ENABLED feature flag.
  2. Enable in staging first; reconcile with provider dashboards for 1 week.
  3. Roll out to production with alerting on missing usage payloads.

docs.title: Cost Reconciliation Workflow
docs.text:
• Daily batch compares internal aggregated cost to provider invoice API.
  • If variance >2%, emit finance alert and lock optimization recommendations until corrected.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
• Metrics: token_usage_capture_rate, usage_parse_failures, cost_variance_pct.
  • Dashboards by provider/model/stage.

docs.title: Ownership Model
docs.text:
• Platform team owns parser + pricing catalog.
  • Product team owns user-facing reports and budget controls.

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Reduce plan timeframes by automatically identifying tasks that can run in parallel, splitting tasks into smaller work packs, and introducing controlled redundancy and PM overhead (“fast-tracking”).

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Many plans are sequential by default. Real projects compress timelines by parallelizing and managing dependencies aggressively.

docs.title: 1) Dependency-aware packing
docs.text:
• Take the WBS + dependencies and compute critical path.

  • Identify tasks off the critical path that can be parallelized.

  • Recommend a packed schedule with parallel lanes.

docs.title: 2) Task splitting
docs.text:
• If a task is long and blocks successors, split it into smaller deliverables:

  • e.g., “Design” → “Design v0”, “Design review”, “Design v1”

  • Allow overlap: start implementation against v0 with rollback/iteration buffer.

docs.title: 3) Redundancy where beneficial
docs.text:
• Duplicate discovery/research tasks across subteams to reduce risk of single-threaded delays.

  • Add explicit “merge + reconcile” tasks.

docs.title: Output additions
docs.text:
• “Parallelization Opportunities” section

  • “Fast-track schedule” Gantt view (baseline vs accelerated)

  • Risk notes: increased coordination + rework probability

docs.title: Algorithm sketch
docs.text:
• Compute earliest start/latest finish

  • Mark critical path

  • For non-critical tasks, pack into parallel lanes by resource class

docs.title: Resource Capacity Assessment (User Interaction)
docs.text: Parallelization is only credible if the planner understands the team’s real capacity. This requires a structured interaction with the user who created the plan to capture resource limits and constraints before the fast-track schedule is produced.

docs.title: What We Need To Ask
docs.text:
Collect a minimal, structured resource profile:

  • Team size by role: engineering, design, ops, compliance, procurement, field staff.
  • Availability windows: hours/week and key blackout periods.
  • Critical shared resources: single points of failure (e.g., one QA lead).
  • Budget limits: ability to hire contractors or add shifts.
  • Coordination overhead tolerance: willingness to accept rework risk.
  • Dependencies on external parties: vendors, regulators, partners.

docs.title: Interaction Flow
docs.text:
1. Present the baseline schedule and highlight critical path constraints.
  2. Ask targeted capacity questions only for roles on the critical path.
  3. Quantify parallelization headroom (e.g., “We can run 2 work packs in parallel for engineering, but only 1 for compliance”).
  4. Confirm trade-offs (speed vs rework vs cost).
  5. Lock a capacity profile that drives the fast-track algorithm.

docs.title: Example Prompt Snippet
docs.text:
We can shorten the schedule by parallelizing tasks. Please confirm:
- Engineering capacity: __ people, __ hrs/week
- Design capacity: __ people, __ hrs/week
- Compliance/legal capacity: __ people, __ hrs/week
- Are you willing to add contractors to speed up? (yes/no)
- Max acceptable rework risk: low/medium/high

docs.title: Output From The Assessment
docs.text:
The system should produce a normalized resource profile, for example:

{
  "roles": {
    "engineering": {"fte": 4, "hours_per_week": 160},
    "design": {"fte": 1, "hours_per_week": 40},
    "compliance": {"fte": 0.5, "hours_per_week": 20}
  },
  "contractor_budget": 50000,
  "rework_risk_tolerance": "medium",
  "external_dependencies": ["regulator_review", "vendor_lead_time"]
}

This assessment becomes the constraint set for the parallelization algorithm and is referenced in the final Gantt output.

docs.title: Success metrics
docs.text:
• Median planned duration reduction (baseline vs fast-track)

  • Rework rate estimate + mitigation completeness

docs.title: 1) Build a scheduling core with dual outputs
docs.text:
For each plan, generate two schedules: 1. Baseline schedule (current dependency-respecting sequence) 2. Accelerated schedule (parallel-packed fast-track)

Store both as first-class artifacts so users can compare tradeoffs.

docs.title: 2) Dependency graph normalization
docs.text:
Parse WBS tasks into DAG nodes: - task_id, duration_estimate, resource_class, depends_on - normalize missing fields with defaults + confidence labels

Run validation: - detect cycles - detect orphan tasks - detect impossible predecessors

docs.title: 3) Critical-path + slack analysis
docs.text:
Compute earliest start, latest finish, total float.

Rules: - Critical path tasks (float=0) are primary compression targets. - Non-critical tasks with high float become parallelization candidates.

docs.title: 4) Fast-track transformations
docs.text: Apply deterministic transformation operators: 1. Split-long-task when duration exceeds threshold and has high blocking impact. 2. Overlap-safe-pairs where partial deliverables can unblock downstream work. 3. Parallel-pack tasks with non-overlapping dependencies and compatible resources. 4. Inject-merge-task after redundant/parallel workstreams.

docs.title: 5) Resource-constrained packing
docs.text:
Use user capacity profile as hard constraints: - max concurrent FTE per role - external bottlenecks (regulatory, vendor windows) - overtime/contractor allowance

Suggested solver approach: - heuristic first-fit decreasing by criticality - optional CP-SAT/ILP mode for high-stakes plans

docs.title: 6) Risk-aware acceleration scoring
docs.text:
Every acceleration action gets risk deltas: - coordination risk - rework probability - quality degradation probability

Compute: net_benefit = schedule_days_saved - risk_penalty_weighted

Only apply changes with positive net benefit unless user opts into aggressive mode.

docs.title: 7) Output format
docs.text: Add sections to plan output: - Parallelization opportunities (with rationale) - Baseline vs fast-track Gantt delta - Resource stress table - Rework-risk heatmap - Recommended governance cadence (daily standups, weekly integration reviews)

docs.title: 8) Integration points
docs.text:
• Hook after initial WBS generation and dependency extraction.
  • Feed accelerated schedule into finance modules (faster schedule may increase labor/coordination costs).
  • Expose mode flag: schedule_mode=baseline|fast_track|aggressive.

docs.title: 9) Rollout phases
docs.text:
• Phase A: analysis-only (no modifications, just suggestions)
  • Phase B: auto-generate accelerated schedule
  • Phase C: add optimization solver + risk scoring
  • Phase D: closed-loop calibration from actual project outcomes

docs.title: 10) Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Synthetic DAG benchmarks with known optimal schedules
  • Regression tests on common project patterns
  • Stress tests for 1k+ task plans
  • User acceptance tests on readability of baseline vs accelerated outputs

docs.title: Scheduling Strategy Modes
docs.text:
• conservative: minimal overlap, low rework risk
  • balanced: moderate overlap, default mode
  • aggressive: max overlap with explicit risk acceptance

docs.title: Critical Path Compression Playbook
docs.text:
1. Detect top 3 blockers by criticality impact.
  2. Apply split/overlap operators.
  3. Recompute with resource constraints.
  4. Accept plan only if risk-adjusted gain remains positive.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
• Require human approval when aggressive mode exceeds rework threshold.
  • Log all schedule transformations for audit.

docs.title: KPI Targets
docs.text:
• =15% median duration reduction in balanced mode

  • <=10% increase in predicted rework cost

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Automatically generate a Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS) from a plan, mapping scope to cost categories, subcategories, and line items with assumptions and confidence levels.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
Most plans mention costs but do not structure them. A CBS enables:

  • Comparable cost estimates across plans.
  • Immediate visibility into cost drivers.
  • Faster budgeting, funding, and procurement decisions.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Without a CBS:

  • Cost claims are vague or non-auditable.
  • Missing categories create hidden risk.
  • Downstream financial models are inconsistent.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Implement a CBS generator that:

  1. Parses plan scope and milestones.
  2. Maps scope elements to standard cost categories.
  3. Produces a multi-level CBS with assumptions and ranges.
  4. Assigns confidence and missing-info flags.

docs.title: CBS Taxonomy (Default)
docs.text:
Level 1 categories:

  • Labor
  • Materials
  • Equipment
  • Software and Licenses
  • Facilities
  • Professional Services
  • Compliance and Legal
  • Operations and Maintenance
  • Contingency

Level 2 examples:

  • Labor: engineering, project management, field staff
  • Materials: raw materials, components, consumables
  • Facilities: rent, utilities, site prep
  • Compliance: permits, audits, regulatory fees

docs.title: 1) Scope Extraction
docs.text:
Identify:

  • Deliverables (what will be built or delivered)
  • Work packages (tasks and milestones)
  • Dependencies and external services

docs.title: 2) Mapping Rules
docs.text:
Apply mapping from scope to cost categories:

  • Physical deliverables -> materials + equipment + labor
  • Software deliverables -> labor + cloud + licenses
  • Regulated projects -> compliance + legal

docs.title: 3) Cost Estimation
docs.text:
Use a combination of:

  • Benchmark ratios (per unit, per employee, per square meter)
  • Historical PlanExe costs
  • User-provided or inferred quantities

docs.title: 3.1) Multi-Currency Handling
docs.text:
Plans may involve multiple currencies (e.g., cross-border bridge projects). The CBS should:

  • Capture line items in their native currency.
  • Store a reporting currency for rollups (default to plan base currency).
  • Record FX assumptions (rate, date, source, volatility band).
  • Allow dual-currency rollups when contracts are split by jurisdiction.

docs.title: 4) Confidence Assignment
docs.text:
• High: explicit quantities and pricing provided.
  • Medium: benchmark-based estimates.
  • Low: inferred or missing data.

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "cbs": [
    {
      "category": "Labor",
      "subcategories": [
        {"name": "Engineering", "estimate": 420000, "currency": "EUR", "confidence": "medium"},
        {"name": "Project Management", "estimate": 120000, "currency": "EUR", "confidence": "medium"}
      ]
    },
    {
      "category": "Compliance and Legal",
      "subcategories": [
        {"name": "Permits", "estimate": 30000, "currency": "DKK", "confidence": "low"}
      ]
    }
  ],
  "total_estimate": 570000,
  "reporting_currency": "EUR",
  "fx_assumptions": [
    {"pair": "DKK/EUR", "rate": 0.13, "as_of": "2026-02-10", "volatility": "medium"}
  ],
  "contingency": 0.12,
  "assumptions": [
    "Engineering team of 5 for 12 months",
    "Permit costs based on regional averages"
  ]
}

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Feed into top-down and bottom-up finance modules.
  • Use as a checklist for missing cost categories.
  • Provide input to bid pricing and risk analysis.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• % plans with a generated CBS.
  • Reduction in unaccounted cost categories during review.
  • Alignment between CBS totals and final budget.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Over-simplified categories: mitigate with domain-specific mappings.
  • False precision: provide ranges and confidence labels.
  • Missing quantities: require user clarification prompts.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Domain-specific CBS templates.
  • Automated cost library updates.
  • Integration with procurement and supplier pricing feeds.

docs.title: 1) Canonical CBS taxonomy service
docs.text:
Create a versioned taxonomy module: - global categories (L1) - domain-specific subcategories (L2/L3) - mapping aliases (e.g., “permits” -> compliance/legal)

This avoids inconsistent CBS labels across plans.

docs.title: 2) WBS-to-CBS mapper
docs.text:
Implement deterministic + ML-assisted mapper: 1. Rule-based first pass from task metadata and keywords. 2. LLM-assisted classification for ambiguous tasks. 3. Confidence score and explanation per mapping.

Store mapping artifacts: - wbs_task_id - cbs_path - mapping_confidence - mapping_reason

docs.title: 3) Cost line generation
docs.text:
For each mapped task, generate cost lines: - quantity - unit - unit rate - currency - low/base/high estimate - source (user input, benchmark, quote, inferred)

Represent uncertainty explicitly; avoid single-point false precision.

docs.title: 4) Assumptions and provenance registry
docs.text:
Every cost line should reference an assumption record: - assumption text - evidence source - owner - last update timestamp

Provide “assumption drift” detection if benchmarks change.

docs.title: 5) CBS outputs and exports
docs.text: Generate: - hierarchical CBS table - WBS↔CBS crosswalk table - top cost drivers with sensitivity - export to CSV/XLSX/JSON for finance tooling

docs.title: 6) Integration with top-down and bottom-up finance
docs.text:
• Top-down uses CBS categories for ratio application.
  • Bottom-up consumes CBS line items as task-level cost ledger.
  • Reconciliation reports highlight CBS categories causing variance.

docs.title: 7) API contract proposal
docs.text:
{
  "plan_id": "...",
  "cbs_version": "v1.0",
  "currency": "USD",
  "items": [
    {
      "wbs_task_id": "3.2",
      "cbs_path": "Labor/Engineering/Backend",
      "estimate": {"low": 50000, "base": 70000, "high": 95000},
      "confidence": "medium",
      "assumption_id": "asm_42"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: 8) Rollout phases
docs.text:
• Phase A: rule-based mapping + static taxonomy
  • Phase B: confidence scoring + assumption registry
  • Phase C: export + integration with finance modules
  • Phase D: live vendor pricing and automated refresh

docs.title: 9) Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Coverage: % WBS tasks mapped to valid CBS nodes
  • Consistency: repeated runs produce stable mappings
  • Auditability: every line has source + assumption
  • Usability: finance users can edit/approve CBS quickly

docs.title: Domain Template Strategy
docs.text:
• Start with 5 templates: software, infra, manufacturing, nonprofit, public sector.
  • Fallback to generic taxonomy when domain confidence < threshold.

docs.title: Editing Workflow
docs.text:
1. Auto-generate CBS draft.
  2. User reviews low-confidence lines first.
  3. Finance reviewer signs off final CBS baseline.

docs.title: Change Control
docs.text:
• Every CBS edit creates a diff record with rationale.
  • Lock finalized CBS version for downstream reconciliation.

docs.title: Export Targets
docs.text:
• CSV for analysts
  • XLSX for procurement/accounting
  • JSON for API consumers

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Provide a fast, defensible financial estimate using market-level benchmarks and macro ratios when bottom-up data is missing. This produces a first-pass budget, revenue, and margin model with explicit confidence bands, enabling early decision-making and investor screening.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
Many plans arrive with limited financial detail. Top-down estimation lets PlanExe:

  • Produce a credible early-stage financial model fast.
  • Identify whether a plan is even plausible before spending time on bottom-up detail.
  • Set guardrails for later bottom-up estimates and reconcile divergences.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
Without a structured top-down pass:

  • Early financials are either missing or invented.
  • Investors cannot compare apples-to-apples across plan proposals.
  • Budget and revenue claims drift far from industry reality.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Implement a top-down estimation module that:

  1. Classifies the plan into a domain and business model archetype.
  2. Pulls benchmark ratios (revenue/employee, gross margin ranges, CAC:LTV, capex intensity).
  3. Uses macro inputs (TAM/SAM/SOM, price points, addressable volume) to estimate revenue.
  4. Produces a multi-year financial model with ranges and confidence levels.
  5. Outputs assumptions and evidence sources for auditability.

docs.title: 1) Domain and Model Classification
docs.text:
Determine the plan's category and model type:

  • Domain: SaaS, consumer apps, logistics, infrastructure, energy, public-sector, etc.
  • Model: subscription, transaction, licensing, service-based, PPP/concession.

docs.title: 2) Benchmark Ratios
docs.text:
Select ratios from sector data:

  • Revenue per employee
  • Gross margin ranges
  • EBITDA margin ranges
  • Sales efficiency (CAC payback, LTV:CAC)
  • Capex as % of revenue
  • Working capital cycles

docs.title: 3) Market Sizing Inputs
docs.text:
Require at least one of:

  • TAM/SAM/SOM estimates
  • Price x volume assumptions
  • Comparable market size and penetration rates

docs.title: 4) Revenue Model
docs.text:
Compute revenue using a constrained top-down approach:

  • Estimate initial penetration rate (low/medium/high) based on stage.
  • Constrain growth rates to sector typical ranges.
  • Generate base, conservative, and aggressive scenarios.

docs.title: 5) Cost Structure
docs.text:
Apply benchmark ratios to revenue:

  • COGS via gross margin range.
  • Opex via typical sales/marketing and R&D ratios.
  • Capex via sector averages and plan type.

docs.title: 6) Output Confidence
docs.text:
Assign a confidence level to each line item based on evidence quality:

  • High: external data or audited inputs.
  • Medium: comparable company benchmarks.
  • Low: assumptions with weak backing.

docs.title: 7) Multi-Currency Handling
docs.text:
Plans may involve multiple currencies (e.g., cross-border bridge projects). The top-down model should:

  • Specify a reporting currency for the consolidated model.
  • Store original currency for localized assumptions.
  • Record FX assumptions (rate, date, source, volatility band).
  • Allow a third currency when local currencies are unstable.

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "model_type": "subscription",
  "domain": "saas",
  "reporting_currency": "USD",
  "fx_assumptions": [
    {"pair": "DKK/USD", "rate": 0.15, "as_of": "2026-02-10", "volatility": "medium"}
  ],
  "assumptions": [
    "SOM = 0.5% of SAM by year 3",
    "Gross margin range 70-85%"
  ],
  "revenue_scenarios": {
    "conservative": [1.2, 2.0, 3.1],
    "base": [1.8, 3.4, 5.6],
    "aggressive": [2.5, 4.8, 7.9]
  },
  "margin_ranges": {
    "gross": [0.70, 0.85],
    "ebitda": [0.10, 0.25]
  },
  "capex_ratio": 0.08,
  "confidence": {
    "revenue": "medium",
    "costs": "medium",
    "capex": "low"
  }
}

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Use in early PlanExe phases when financial data is missing.
  • Feed into risk scoring and investor thesis matching.
  • Compare with bottom-up output in reconciliation stage.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Top-down estimate time under 60 seconds for standard plans.
  • Percentage of plans with top-down model generated.
  • Variance between top-down and bottom-up within acceptable bands.
  • Investor feedback: perceived credibility of early-stage financials.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Over-reliance on weak benchmarks: mitigate with confidence labels.
  • Domain mismatch: mitigate with explicit classification step.
  • False precision: mitigate by publishing ranges, not single-point estimates.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Automated sourcing of sector benchmarks.
  • Dynamic calibration from historical PlanExe outcomes.
  • Integrate sensitivity analysis and scenario shock testing.

docs.title: 1) Build a benchmark intelligence layer
docs.text:
Create a benchmark catalog service keyed by: - domain - business model - geography - project scale - maturity stage

Each benchmark entry includes: - value range (P10/P50/P90) - source and freshness - confidence and applicability notes

docs.title: 2) Plan classification pipeline
docs.text:
Before estimation: 1. classify plan domain/model 2. detect geography and currency context 3. infer scale band (small/medium/large)

Classification drives benchmark retrieval and confidence scoring.

docs.title: 3) Top-down estimate engine
docs.text:
Compute revenue/cost envelopes from benchmark priors: - market sizing assumptions (TAM/SAM/SOM) - penetration trajectory - ratio-driven opex/capex

Output three scenarios: - conservative - base - aggressive

and include explicit assumptions per scenario.

docs.title: 4) Confidence computation
docs.text:
Confidence should be model-based, not narrative: - data completeness score - benchmark relevance score - volatility score for domain/region

confidence_index = completeness * relevance * (1 - volatility_penalty)

docs.title: 5) Guardrail rules
docs.text:
Add hard checks: - growth rates outside realistic domain ranges - gross margins incompatible with business model - capex intensity outlier flags

When violated, annotate with corrective recommendations.

docs.title: 6) Integration and outputs
docs.text:
• Save top-down artifact as structured JSON
  • Generate markdown narrative for plan report
  • Feed into reconciliation module (Proposal 35)
  • Feed risk engine with high-variance assumptions

docs.title: 7) Rollout phases
docs.text:
• Phase A: static benchmark tables + deterministic formulas
  • Phase B: dynamic benchmark retrieval + confidence scoring
  • Phase C: sensitivity analysis (1-way + multi-factor)
  • Phase D: automatic calibration from completed project outcomes

docs.title: 8) Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Benchmark coverage by domain/model
  • Stability across reruns with same inputs
  • Human reviewer agreement on plausibility
  • Delta to bottom-up within target tolerance bands

docs.title: Benchmark Lifecycle
docs.text:
1. Ingest benchmark sources weekly.
  2. Version benchmark snapshots.
  3. Track drift in benchmark medians and ranges.

docs.title: Estimation Safety Rules
docs.text:
• Always emit ranges (never single-point only).
  • Down-rank confidence when source freshness exceeds SLA.
  • Flag plans with assumptions outside benchmark confidence intervals.

docs.title: Review Loop
docs.text:
• Finance reviewer can override assumptions with justification.
  • Overrides are logged and fed into calibration analytics.

docs.title: Calibration KPI
docs.text:
• Mean absolute percentage error vs realized outcomes
  • Target: trend down quarter-over-quarter

docs.title: Finance Analysis via Bottom-Up Estimation + Reconciliation
Pitch
docs.text: Build a bottom-up financial model from tasks, resources, and unit economics, then reconcile it against top-down estimates to surface gaps and improve accuracy.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Top-down estimates are fast but coarse. Bottom-up estimates are realistic but time-consuming. Combining both gives the speed of top-down with the credibility of bottom-up, while exposing unrealistic assumptions early.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
• Plans often include partial or inconsistent financials.
  • Bottom-up models are missing or unstructured.
  • Divergence between top-down and bottom-up is not tracked.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Implement a bottom-up estimation module that:

  1. Extracts work packages, resources, and timelines.
  2. Builds cost and revenue from unit-level assumptions.
  3. Aggregates to totals and cash flow.
  4. Reconciles differences with top-down estimates.

docs.title: 1) Work Package Extraction
docs.text:
Identify:

  • Tasks and milestones
  • Deliverables and work packages
  • Staffing requirements
  • Duration and dependencies

docs.title: 2) Unit Cost Modeling
docs.text:
Attach costs per unit:

  • Labor: role-based hourly or monthly rates
  • Materials: quantity x price
  • Infrastructure: cloud usage, hardware
  • External services: contractors, vendors

docs.title: 3) Revenue Modeling
docs.text:
Build revenue from:

  • Units sold x price
  • Contract values and timelines
  • Subscription tiers and churn
  • Conversion funnel estimates

docs.title: 4) Aggregation
docs.text:
Produce:

  • Project budget by phase
  • Monthly burn and runway
  • Break-even timing
  • Profit and loss summary

docs.title: 5) Multi-Currency Handling
docs.text:
Plans may involve multiple currencies (e.g., cross-border projects). The bottom-up model should:

  • Track line items in native currency at the work-package level.
  • Roll up to a reporting currency with explicit FX assumptions.
  • Support a third currency when local currencies are unstable.

docs.title: Reconciliation Layer
docs.text:
Compare bottom-up vs top-down outputs:

  • Total revenue variance
  • Margin variance
  • Capex and opex mismatches
  • Timeline inconsistencies

Reconciliation output:

  • Variance report
  • Recommended adjustments
  • Updated confidence levels

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "bottom_up": {
    "total_cost": 2200000,
    "total_revenue": 4800000,
    "burn_rate_monthly": 180000,
    "reporting_currency": "USD",
    "fx_assumptions": [
      {"pair": "BRL/USD", "rate": 0.19, "as_of": "2026-02-10", "volatility": "high"}
    ]
  },
  "top_down": {
    "total_cost": 1500000,
    "total_revenue": 5200000
  },
  "variance": {
    "cost_delta": 700000,
    "revenue_delta": -400000
  },
  "reconciliation_notes": [
    "Bottom-up assumes 12 engineers, top-down assumes 8",
    "Top-down margin range exceeds observed unit economics"
  ]
}

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Uses CBS generation as input for cost categories.
  • Feeds into investor thesis matching and risk scoring.
  • Drives evidence-based adjustments in financial claims.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Percentage of plans with bottom-up models.
  • Reduction in financial variance after reconciliation.
  • Investor confidence in financial projections.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• High data requirements: mitigate with default benchmarks and missing info prompts.
  • Estimation complexity: prioritize major cost drivers first.
  • False precision: publish ranges and confidence scores.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Automated cost libraries by region and sector.
  • Sensitivity analysis and scenario modeling.
  • Learning system that updates estimates from real outcomes.

docs.title: 1) Bottom-up estimator architecture
docs.text:
For each WBS task, build a cost object: - labor profile (roles, hours, rates) - material BOM (qty × unit cost) - external services - fixed/variable overhead - contingency allocation

Aggregate task costs -> work package -> phase -> plan total.

docs.title: 2) Revenue build-up layer
docs.text:
For plans with revenue: - unit sales model or contract milestone model - churn/renewal assumptions (if subscription) - conversion and ramp assumptions

Link revenue timing to project timeline for cashflow realism.

docs.title: 3) Reconciliation algorithm (top-down vs bottom-up)
docs.text:
Input: - top-down scenario bands (P10/P50/P90) - bottom-up deterministic/ranged total

Compute variance decomposition by category: - labor delta - materials delta - capex delta - schedule-induced delta

Generate reconciliation recommendations ranked by expected impact.

docs.title: 4) Convergence rules
docs.text:
Define explicit convergence status: - green: variance <= 10% - yellow: 10–20% - red: >20%

If yellow/red, require iteration actions before “finance-ready” status.

docs.title: 5) Iteration loop
docs.text:
1. identify highest variance categories
  2. request missing inputs or benchmark corrections
  3. update assumptions
  4. recompute both models
  5. re-evaluate convergence state

Track each iteration for audit.

docs.title: 6) Integration points
docs.text:
• Pull CBS line items from Proposal 33
  • Pull benchmark priors from Proposal 34
  • Expose convergence status in plan summary
  • Feed risk module when persistent red variance remains

docs.title: 7) Output package
docs.text: Produce a finance bundle: - bottom-up ledger - top-down summary - variance decomposition chart - reconciliation action log - convergence status + signoff checklist

docs.title: 8) Rollout phases
docs.text:
• Phase A: deterministic bottom-up + simple variance
  • Phase B: ranged bottom-up with confidence levels
  • Phase C: automated reconciliation recommendations
  • Phase D: closed-loop learning from actual spend/revenue outcomes

docs.title: 9) Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Accounting consistency (totals match component sums)
  • Reproducibility under fixed assumptions
  • Reviewer confidence uplift after reconciliation
  • Reduced forecast error on executed projects

docs.title: Convergence Workflow
docs.text:
1. Generate bottom-up ledger from WBS/CBS.
  2. Pull top-down baseline from proposal 34 module.
  3. Compute variance by category and timeline bucket.
  4. Trigger correction cycle until convergence status reaches green/yellow threshold.

docs.title: Action Prioritization
docs.text:
• Rank correction actions by expected variance reduction per effort unit.
  • Recommend max 5 actions per cycle to avoid analysis overload.

docs.title: Signoff Policy
docs.text:
• Red variance blocks investor-ready status.
  • Yellow requires explicit financial waiver.
  • Green auto-advances to packaging phase.

docs.title: Learning Loop
docs.text: • Persist convergence trajectories to improve future default assumptions.

docs.title: Monte Carlo Plan Success Probability Engine
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-11 Status: Proposal Audience: Data Scientists, Project Managers

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Monte Carlo Plan Success Engine moves planning from deterministic "best guesses" to probabilistic distributions. By running 10,000 stochastic simulations of the project schedule and budget, it provides a mathematical confidence level for success (e.g., "There is a 12% chance of finishing by Q3").

It replaces single-point estimates (e.g., "This task takes 5 days") with probability distributions (e.g., "Between 3 and 10 days, most likely 5").

docs.title: Core Problem
docs.text: "The Flaw of Averages": The average outcome of a project is worse than the plan constructed from average inputs, due to non-linear dependencies (Jensen's Inequality). Deterministic plans systematically underestimate risk.

docs.title: 1. Distribution Modeler
docs.text: Converts scalar plan inputs into statistical distributions. - Tasks: Beta-PERT distribution ($O + 4M + P / 6$) for duration. - Costs: Lognormal distribution (skewed right) for budget items. - Risks: Bernoulli trials ($p$) with impact distributions ($I$).

docs.title: 2. Simulation Loop (The Engine)
docs.text: Runs $N$ iterations (default 10,000). For each iteration $i$: 1. Sample duration $d_{i,t}$ for every task $t$. 2. Sample cost $c_{i,b}$ for every budget line $b$. 3. Trigger risks based on probability. 4. Recompute Critical Path ($CP_i$) and Total Cost ($TC_i$). 5. Store pair $(CP_i, TC_i)$.

docs.title: 3. Analytics Service
docs.text: Aggregates the simulation results into interpretable metrics (P10, P50, P90).

docs.title: Input Distributions
docs.text:
Most task durations follow a Beta-PERT distribution: $$E = \frac{Optimistic + 4 \cdot MostLikely + Pessimistic}{6}$$ $$Var = \left(\frac{Pessimistic - Optimistic}{6}\right)^2$$

Cost inputs often follow a Lognormal distribution: $$f(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ This reflects the reality that costs can explode (10x overrun) but rarely shrink below a floor.

docs.title: The Simulation Algorithm
docs.text:
def run_simulation(plan, n_iterations=10000):
    results = []
    for _ in range(n_iterations):
        scenario = {}
        # 1. Sample Tasks
        for task in plan.tasks:
            duration = sample_pert(task.min, task.mode, task.max)
            scenario[task.id] = duration

        # 2. Trigger Risks
        for risk in plan.risks:
            if random.random() < risk.probability:
                # Add delay to linked tasks
                for target in risk.impacts:
                     scenario[target] += risk.delay_days

        # 3. Solve Critical Path
        finish_date = solve_cpm(plan.dependencies, scenario)

        results.append(finish_date)

    return aggregate_stats(results)

docs.title: Tornado Charts
docs.text: We calculate the correlation coefficient between each task's duration and the total project duration. - High Correlation: This task is a "Driver". Focusing on it reduces overall variance. - Low Correlation: This task has "Slack". Delays here likely won't hurt the project.

docs.title: Probabilistic S-Curves
docs.text: A plot of $Cumulative Probability$ vs $Time/Cost$. - P50 (Median): The "Coin Flip" outcome. - P80 (Commitment): The recommended internal deadline. - P95 (Safe Bet): The recommended external promise.

docs.title: Assumption Drift Monitor
docs.text: When drift is detected (e.g., "Steel prices up 10%"), the Monte Carlo engine automatically re-simulates using the new actuals as the baseline, updating the P-values in real-time.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
POST /api/simulate/plan/{id} Trigger a full 10k run. 

{
  "iterations": 10000,
  "seed": 42
}

GET /api/simulate/results/{id} Get the distribution data. 

{
  "p10_date": "2026-06-01",
  "p50_date": "2026-07-15",
  "p90_date": "2026-09-01",
  "success_prob_deadline": 0.42 # 42% chance to hit deadline
}

docs.title: Phase 1: Simulation Foundation (2–3 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Define normalized simulation input contract:
  2. task duration distributions (optimistic/most likely/pessimistic)
  3. cost uncertainty distributions per CBS line item

  4. risk event probabilities and impact models

  5. Build Monte Carlo core package:

  6. deterministic seed support for reproducibility
  7. vectorized simulation runner (NumPy/JAX-compatible)

  8. scenario persistence for post-run diagnostics

  9. Add baseline output artifacts:

  10. percentile summaries (P10/P50/P90)
  11. success probability against target date/cost
  12. critical-path frequency map

docs.title: Phase 2: Pipeline Integration (2 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Add a post-planning stage in run_plan_pipeline.py:
  2. simulate_plan_success

  3. consumes WBS, CBS, risk register

  4. Persist results in structured storage:

  5. run-level summary table

  6. optional per-iteration table for top-N scenario replay

  7. Expose API and report sections:

  8. /api/simulate/plan/{id} trigger endpoint
  9. /api/simulate/results/{id} retrieval endpoint
  10. markdown report block in generated plan output

docs.title: Phase 3: Calibration + Reliability (2–4 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Backtest against historical completed projects.
  2. Calibrate distribution parameters by domain.
  3. Add quality gates:
  4. minimum input completeness threshold

  5. confidence labels (high/medium/low) by data quality

  6. Add drift detection:

  7. trigger re-sim when assumptions or benchmark inputs change.

docs.title: Data and API contracts
docs.text:
Suggested summary payload extension:

{
  "simulation": {
    "iterations": 10000,
    "seed": 42,
    "success_probability": 0.42,
    "schedule": {"p10": "2026-06-01", "p50": "2026-07-15", "p90": "2026-09-01"},
    "cost": {"p10": 1200000, "p50": 1500000, "p90": 2100000},
    "top_drivers": ["foundation_work", "steel_supply", "permit_delay"]
  }
}

docs.title: Operational safeguards
docs.text:
• Hard cap on iterations for interactive usage.
  • Queue long simulations asynchronously.
  • Cache deterministic runs by (plan_hash, assumptions_hash, seed).

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Unit tests for distribution samplers.
  • Statistical sanity tests (mean/variance bounds).
  • Integration tests for API + report rendering.
  • Backtest acceptance criteria per domain.

docs.title: Compute Strategy
docs.text:
• Interactive mode: 1,000 runs for quick feedback
  • Batch mode: 10,000+ runs for decision-grade outputs
  • Queue heavy runs with cancellation support

docs.title: Reliability Controls
docs.text:
• Seeded reproducibility for audit
  • Input validation for distribution parameter sanity
  • Guardrails against invalid dependency graphs

docs.title: Explainability
docs.text:
• Output top driver contributions per percentile shift
  • Include “what changed this outcome” narrative snippets

docs.title: SLOs
docs.text:
• P95 interactive response < 8s (1k runs)
  • Batch completion < 2 min for standard plan size

docs.title: Cashflow + Funding Stress Monte Carlo (How Money Moves)
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-10 Status: Proposal Tags: cashflow, finance, simulation, liquidity, risk

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Simulate weekly or monthly cash movement under uncertainty to identify liquidity cliffs, funding gaps, and insolvency windows before execution starts.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Projects fail from cash timing issues even when total budget looks sufficient. A stress simulation surfaces liquidity risk early and informs funding structure.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
• Budget totals do not capture timing risk.
  • Payment delays and drawdown constraints are often ignored.
  • Financing plans are rarely stress-tested.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Build a Monte Carlo cashflow simulator that:

  1. Models inflows and outflows over time.
  2. Incorporates stochastic delays and default probabilities.
  3. Runs thousands of scenarios to estimate liquidity risk.
  4. Produces funding buffer recommendations.

docs.title: Inflows
docs.text:
• milestone payments
  • investor tranches
  • grants
  • debt drawdowns

docs.title: Outflows
docs.text:
• labor and contractors
  • materials and equipment
  • logistics
  • compliance and legal
  • contingency

docs.title: Risk Drivers
docs.text:
• counterparty payment delays
  • procurement cost inflation
  • FX volatility (for multi-currency plans)
  • timeline slips affecting cash burn

docs.title: Simulation Workflow
docs.text:
1. Build baseline cashflow schedule.
  2. Sample stochastic events (delays, cost spikes).
  3. Compute cash balance over time.
  4. Record insolvency windows and buffer needs.

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "probability_negative_cash": 0.27,
  "min_cash_buffer": 1800000,
  "worst_case_gap": 3200000,
  "time_to_insolvency_weeks": 14
}

docs.title: Policy Hooks
docs.text:
• Block plan escalation if liquidity failure probability exceeds threshold.
  • Recommend tranche redesign or payment renegotiation.
  • Adjust schedule to smooth peak burn periods.

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Feeds into top-down and bottom-up finance modules.
  • Informs investor risk scoring and funding structure.
  • Links to risk propagation network.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Reduction in mid-project funding crises.
  • Better alignment between payment schedules and burn.
  • Increased confidence in funding adequacy.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Over-reliance on assumed distributions.
  • Underestimating black swan funding shocks.
  • Poor quality input data yields false security.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Scenario-specific macro stress models.
  • Automated FX hedging analysis.
  • Live cashflow tracking during execution.

docs.title: Phase 1: Cashflow Model Assembly
docs.text:
1. Build canonical cashflow timeline object:
  2. period granularity (weekly/monthly)
  3. inflow schedule with uncertainty bands

  4. outflow schedule from CBS and staffing plans

  5. Add uncertainty injectors:

  6. receivable delay distributions
  7. procurement inflation shocks
  8. FX movement models for multi-currency projects

  9. drawdown timing constraints for debt/grants

  10. Define insolvency rules:

  11. threshold crossing (cash_balance < 0)
  12. sustained shortfall windows (n periods below minimum buffer)

docs.title: Phase 2: Stress Simulation Engine
docs.text:
1. Run 10,000 stochastic scenarios with deterministic seed option.
  2. Compute key outputs:
  3. probability of negative cash by period
  4. minimum viable reserve buffer

  5. required funding bridge amount and timing

  6. Tag scenario archetypes:

  7. delay-driven insolvency
  8. inflation-driven insolvency
  9. FX-driven insolvency

docs.title: Phase 3: Decision Layer + Policy Hooks
docs.text:
1. Add policy thresholds configurable by domain/risk appetite.
  2. Emit recommendations automatically:
  3. resequence payment milestones
  4. increase contingency reserve

  5. add backup credit facility

  6. Integrate with bid/no-bid gate:

  7. block escalation if liquidity failure probability exceeds limit.

docs.title: Data model additions
docs.text:
• cashflow_scenarios (run_id, period, inflow, outflow, balance, scenario_id)
  • cashflow_risk_summary (run_id, p_negative_cash, min_buffer, worst_gap)
  • funding_actions (run_id, action_type, expected_impact)

docs.title: UX/reporting
docs.text: Add a dedicated report section: - cash-at-risk curve - highest-risk periods - mitigation playbook with expected probability reduction

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Reconcile baseline simulation with deterministic cashflow model.
  • Verify multi-currency translation consistency.
  • Backtest against historical liquidity incidents where available.

docs.title: Treasury Simulation Features
docs.text:
• Dynamic cash floor policy per project stage
  • Payment delay distributions by counterparty type
  • Optional emergency facility simulation

docs.title: Decision Outputs
docs.text:
• Minimum reserve recommendation
  • Funding bridge trigger points
  • Suggested payment milestone re-shaping

docs.title: Alerting
docs.text:
• Critical alert when insolvency probability exceeds configured threshold
  • Daily digest for plans in warning zone

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Replay historical near-insolvency projects for calibration
  • Stress test with correlated shock scenarios

docs.title: Risk Propagation Network + Failure Mode Manifestation
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-10 Status: Proposal Tags: risk, propagation, failure-modes, simulation, dependencies

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Model how local risks propagate through dependencies to system-level failure, then simulate manifestation paths across many runs to surface the most likely cascades and highest-leverage interventions.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Risks rarely fail in isolation. Large project failures typically emerge from interacting risks across domains (technical, procurement, financing, regulatory). A propagation model makes these interactions explicit and actionable.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
• Risk registers treat items independently.
  • Teams under-estimate compounding effects.
  • Mitigation choices are not ranked by systemic impact.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Build a Risk Propagation Network that:

  1. Represents risks, tasks, and milestones as a connected graph.
  2. Encodes causal links and delay effects between nodes.
  3. Simulates cascades across the network.
  4. Outputs failure pathways and intervention leverage scores.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Plan JSON
  -> Risk + Dependency Extraction
  -> Propagation Graph Builder
  -> Cascade Simulator (Monte Carlo)
  -> Failure Path Analyzer
  -> Mitigation Prioritizer

docs.title: Graph Model
docs.text:
• Nodes: risks, tasks, milestones, resources.
  • Edges: causal amplification and delay links.
  • Weights: probability impact, lag time, and severity multiplier.

docs.title: Example edge
docs.text:
• Procurement delay -> schedule slippage (weight: high, lag: 2 weeks)
  • Schedule slippage -> financing drawdown risk (weight: medium, lag: 1 month)

docs.title: Simulation
docs.text:
Run multi-step simulations to reveal cascades:

  • Sample risk events based on probability distributions.
  • Propagate effects through graph edges.
  • Track which nodes fail, when, and how often.

Outputs per run:

  • failure sequence
  • time-to-failure
  • cost impact

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "top_failure_paths": [
    {
      "path": ["procurement_delay", "schedule_slip", "financing_gap"],
      "probability": 0.18,
      "expected_loss": 4200000
    }
  ],
  "intervention_points": [
    {"node": "procurement_delay", "leverage": 0.72}
  ]
}

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Feeds into Monte Carlo plan success probability engine.
  • Adds a propagation-adjusted risk score to plan ranking.
  • Triggers mitigation playbooks for top cascades.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Reduction in surprise compound failures.
  • Increased mitigation effectiveness vs baseline risk registers.
  • Improved forecast accuracy for delays and cost overruns.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Model complexity could obscure interpretation.
  • Missing edges lead to false security.
  • Overfitting to historical cascades.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Automated edge discovery from historical plans.
  • Dynamic updates as execution data arrives.
  • Cross-project risk propagation benchmarking.

docs.title: Phase 1: Graph Construction Layer
docs.text:
1. Define canonical node types:

  2. risk_event, task, milestone, resource_constraint

  3. Define edge semantics:

  4. causal amplification
  5. schedule delay transfer
  6. cost transfer

  7. confidence score per edge

  8. Build graph extraction adapters from plan artifacts:

  9. WBS + dependencies
  10. risk register
  11. finance assumptions

docs.title: Phase 2: Propagation Simulator
docs.text:
1. At each simulation tick:
  2. sample active risk events
  3. propagate effects along outgoing edges

  4. update impacted task states and milestone forecasts

  5. Capture cascade traces:

  6. first-trigger node
  7. propagation chain

  8. terminal failure state

  9. Aggregate over 10,000 runs:

  10. pathway frequencies
  11. expected loss per pathway
  12. median time-to-failure

docs.title: Phase 3: Mitigation Optimizer
docs.text:
1. Score intervention points by marginal risk reduction.
  2. Recommend top mitigation portfolio under budget constraints.
  3. Re-simulate with mitigations applied to show deltas.

docs.title: Suggested algorithmic approach
docs.text:
• Use weighted directed graph with event queue.
  • Compute influence centrality to prioritize mitigation.
  • Run counterfactual analysis: remove/attenuate edge and measure probability delta.

docs.title: Data model additions
docs.text:
• risk_graph_nodes (run_id, node_id, node_type, metadata)
  • risk_graph_edges (run_id, src, dst, edge_type, weight, lag)
  • risk_cascade_paths (run_id, path_json, probability, expected_loss)

docs.title: Integration points
docs.text:
• Feed risk pathway penalties into ELO/selection ranking.
  • Push high-risk cascade alerts into governance dashboard.
  • Link mitigation actions back into planning artifacts.

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Synthetic graph tests with known cascades.
  • Stability tests for edge-weight perturbation.
  • Human review of top-10 pathways for interpretability.

docs.title: Graph Build Pipeline
docs.text:
1. Extract nodes from tasks, risks, resources.
  2. Infer edges from dependencies and causal templates.
  3. Score edge confidence and impact weight.

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
• Run cascade simulation alongside baseline Monte Carlo.
  • Persist top failure chains and intervention candidates.

docs.title: Mitigation Planner
docs.text:
• Compute marginal risk reduction for each intervention.
  • Recommend portfolio under mitigation budget constraint.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text: • Require review for interventions with high operational disruption.

docs.title: Frontier Research Gap Mapper for Mega-Projects
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-10 Status: Proposal Tags: research, frontier, megaprojects, feasibility, innovation

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text: Detect where a plan depends on unresolved science or engineering and map those dependencies into a structured R&D register before committing to a bid.

docs.title: Why
docs.text: Some plans require breakthroughs, not just execution discipline. Hidden research dependencies are major bid risks that should be explicit, costed, and staged.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
• Frontier gaps are often implicit, not stated.
  • Feasibility assessments assume mature technology.
  • Bids proceed before critical R&D constraints are understood.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Create a module that:

  1. Identifies plan components that exceed current state-of-practice.
  2. Classifies maturity level and research gaps.
  3. Produces a research dependency register with timelines and uncertainty.
  4. Adjusts bidability and feasibility scores accordingly.

docs.title: Classification Framework
docs.text:
Each component is tagged as:

  • Mature: proven in real-world deployments.
  • Adaptation Required: proven elsewhere but needs modification.
  • Frontier: unproven at required scale or conditions.

docs.title: Research Dependency Register
docs.text:
Each gap includes:

  • Challenge statement
  • Current state-of-practice
  • Missing capability threshold
  • Estimated R&D timeline
  • Cost uncertainty band

docs.title: Example Challenge Classes (Arctic Bridge)
docs.text:
• ultra-cold concrete curing and durability
  • ice-load resistant structural systems
  • remote logistics and year-round constructability
  • cross-border governance and standards harmonization

docs.title: Output Schema
docs.text:
{
  "component": "ice_load_resilience",
  "maturity": "frontier",
  "gap": "No validated structural system for multi-year ice pack",
  "estimated_rnd_years": 3,
  "cost_uncertainty": "high"
}

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text:
• Feeds into risk propagation network and Monte Carlo success probability.
  • Applies bidability penalty for unresolved frontier gaps.
  • Triggers pre-bid R&D phase recommendations.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• Fewer bids on technically premature opportunities.
  • Better planning of R&D-first project phases.
  • Reduced execution failure from unknown technical gaps.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Over-classification of challenges as frontier.
  • Incomplete research signals due to limited sources.
  • R&D timelines difficult to estimate accurately.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
• Automated scanning of research literature and patents.
  • Expert panels for frontier assessment.
  • Continuous updates as research advances.

docs.title: Phase 1: Frontier Detection Framework
docs.text:
1. Build capability taxonomy by domain:
  2. materials science
  3. civil/structural engineering
  4. logistics in extreme environments

  5. cross-border governance and standards

  6. Implement maturity classifier:

  7. mature / adaptation required / frontier

  8. confidence score with evidence references

  9. Attach evidence retrieval:

  10. standards databases
  11. recent publications/patents
  12. comparable projects and postmortems

docs.title: Phase 2: Research Dependency Register
docs.text:
For each frontier dependency, generate: - challenge statement - missing capability threshold - candidate research tracks - expected R&D duration/cost ranges - kill criteria for failed approaches

Store as a first-class artifact linked to plan sections.

docs.title: Phase 3: Bidability and sequencing logic
docs.text:
1. Compute a frontier feasibility index.
  2. Adjust bid/no-bid recommendation based on unresolved high-severity frontier items.
  3. Insert pre-bid R&D phases before main execution phases when required.

docs.title: Example for Bering-style bridge scenario
docs.text:
Unresolved class: cold-climate concrete performance.

System output should include: - what performance threshold is missing - current evidence gap - 3 candidate pathways to close the gap - timeline + budget impact per pathway

docs.title: Data model additions
docs.text:
• frontier_dependencies (plan_id, component, maturity, severity, confidence)
  • research_tracks (dependency_id, hypothesis, timeline_range, cost_range)
  • frontier_index (plan_id, score, blockers_count)

docs.title: Governance hooks
docs.text:
• Require expert signoff for high-severity frontier dependencies.
  • Prevent final “verified” status if critical frontier blockers unresolved.

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Expert review agreement on maturity labels.
  • Correlation between frontier index and downstream execution risk.
  • Reduction in technically premature bids.

docs.title: Frontier Detection Ops
docs.text:
• Build challenge classifiers for materials, environment, logistics, and policy domains.
  • Attach evidence references for each frontier label.

docs.title: Research Program Builder
docs.text:
• Convert frontier gaps into staged R&D tracks with gates:
  • feasibility proof
  • pilot validation
  • scale readiness

docs.title: Bid Integration
docs.text:
• Add bidability penalty for unresolved critical frontier gaps.
  • Surface required pre-bid R&D budget/time in executive summary.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: • Compare mapper outputs with expert panel assessments.

docs.title: Three-Hypotheses Engine for Unsolved Challenges
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-11 Status: Proposal Audience: R&D Leads, Innovation Managers

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Three-Hypotheses Engine formalizes the "Discovery Phase" of a plan. When a team encounters an unsolved technical challenge (a "Known Unknown"), the system enforces a structured approach: generate exactly three viable hypotheses, design experiments to test them, and track progress until one succeeds or all fail.

It prevents "Tunnel Vision" (betting everything on one unproven idea) and "Analysis Paralysis" (endless research without action).

docs.title: Core Problem
docs.text: Innovation is chaotic. Teams often pursue a single "pet theory" for months, only to fail. They rarely define up front what constitutes success or failure, leading to "Zombie Projects" that consume resources but produce no value.

docs.title: 1. Challenge Definition
docs.text: The user defines the Problem Statement (not the solution). * Example: "We need concrete that cures at -30°C."

docs.title: 2. Hypothesis Generator (H1, H2, H3)
docs.text: The system (or user) proposes 3 distinct approaches. Each hypothesis is a "Bet". * H1 (The Favorite): High probability, moderate cost. * H2 (The Backup): Proven tech, higher cost/lower performance. * H3 (The Moonshot): Low probability, game-changing payoff.

docs.title: 3. Experiment Desiuge
docs.text: For each hypothesis, defining the Test Protocol: * Variable: What are we changing? * Metric: What are we measuring? * Success Criteria: What number means "It works"? * Kill Criteria: What number means "Abandon ship"?

docs.title: 4. The Lifecycle Engine
docs.text: A state machine that tracks each hypothesis through its stages: Proposed -> Approved -> Testing -> Validated OR Refuted.

docs.title: The "EVI" Formula (Expected Value of Information)
docs.text:
We prioritize experiments based on EVI.

$$EVI = (P_{success} \times Value_{success}) - Cost_{experiment}$$

Where: - $P_{success}$: Probability the hypothesis is true (Estimated). - $Value_{success}$: The Net Present Value (NPV) of the solution if it works. - $Cost_{experiment}$: The cost to run the test.

Algorithm: 1. Calculate EVI for H1, H2, H3. 2. If EVI(H1) >> EVI(H2), run H1 first (Serial Strategy). 3. If EVI(H1) ≈ EVI(H2), run both (Parallel Strategy).

docs.title: Output Schema (JSON)
docs.text:
The structure of a Challenge object:

{
  "challenge_id": "chal_777",
  "problem_statement": "Cold-weather concrete curing",
  "status": "active",
  "hypotheses": [
    {
      "id": "H1",
      "title": "Chemical Admixture",
      "p_success": 0.6,
      "cost_test": 5000,
      "value_success": 1000000,
      "evi": 595000,
      "state": "testing", 
      "experiments": [
        {
          "id": "exp_1",
          "metric": "cure_time_hours",
          "target": "< 24",
          "result": null
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "H2",
      "title": "Heated Formwork",
      "p_success": 0.9,
      "cost_test": 50000,
      "value_success": 800000, # Lower value due to higher operational cost
      "evi": 670000, 
      "state": "queued"
    }
  ],
  "strategy": "parallel" # Run H1 and H2 because both have high EVI
}

docs.title: User Interface: "The Experiment Dashboard"
docs.text: A Kanban board with a twist. Columns are: 1. Hypotheses: The 3 contenders. 2. In Flight: Active experiments. 3. The Pivot Point: Where decisions happen. 4. Graveyard: Failed hypotheses (with "Post-Mortem" attached). 5. Winner's Circle: The validated solution.

docs.title: The "Kill Switch"
docs.text: If an experiment hits its "Kill Criteria" (e.g., "Cost > $100/unit"), the system automatically flags the hypothesis as Refuted and recommends moving resources to the next one.

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text:
1. Automated Literature Review: Agents that scan arXiv/Patents to suggest H1/H2/H3.
  2. Bayesian Updating: Automatically update $P_{success}$ based on partial experiment results.

docs.title: Phase 1: Hypothesis object model and state machine
docs.text:
1. Define strict schema for each hypothesis:
  2. assumptions
  3. required experiments
  4. expected value of success

  5. kill criteria

  6. Implement lifecycle state machine:

  7. proposed
  8. approved
  9. testing
  10. validated
  11. refuted

  12. archived

  13. Add immutable event log for all state transitions.

docs.title: Phase 2: Experiment protocol builder
docs.text:
For each hypothesis, auto-generate experiment cards: - objective metric - target threshold - sample size / duration - budget cap - stop-loss condition

Require explicit owner and due date before launch.

docs.title: Phase 3: Portfolio execution strategy
docs.text:
1. Compute EVI for H1/H2/H3.
  2. Choose execution mode:
  3. serial if one hypothesis dominates

  4. parallel if top two EVIs are close

  5. Reallocate budget dynamically when a hypothesis is refuted.

docs.title: Phase 4: Bayesian update loop
docs.text: After each experiment result: 1. Update posterior success probability. 2. Recalculate EVI and ranking. 3. Trigger recommendation: - continue - pivot - terminate challenge

docs.title: Data model additions
docs.text:
• challenge_registry (challenge_id, statement, domain, owner, status)
  • hypothesis_cards (challenge_id, hypothesis_id, p_success, evi, state)
  • experiment_runs (hypothesis_id, metrics_json, pass_fail, cost)
  • decision_log (challenge_id, decision, rationale, timestamp)

docs.title: Integration with Monte Carlo and planning
docs.text:
• Inject hypothesis success distributions into Monte Carlo plan simulations.
  • Update plan success probability after each experiment cycle.
  • Surface “research readiness” score in bid/no-bid outputs.

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Time-to-first-validated-hypothesis trend.
  • Reduction in dead-end R&D spend.
  • Post-hoc accuracy of EVI-based prioritization.

docs.title: Hypothesis Portfolio Rules
docs.text:
• Force diversity across H1/H2/H3 approaches.
  • Disallow near-duplicate hypotheses unless justified.

docs.title: Experiment Program Control
docs.text:
• Every hypothesis must have explicit kill criteria.
  • Budget caps enforced per experiment lane.
  • Stop-loss automation when outcomes violate thresholds.

docs.title: Decision Cadence
docs.text:
• Weekly hypothesis review board
  • Auto-update posterior probabilities after each result batch
  • Re-rank hypothesis queue by updated EVI

docs.title: Outcome Tracking
docs.text:
• Measure time-to-first-validated-path
  • Track dead-end spend ratio and reduction over time

docs.title: Autonomous Execution of a Plan (AI + Human Delegation)
Pitch
docs.text: Turn a PlanExe plan into a living execution program that runs autonomously where possible, delegates to humans where necessary, and continuously re-plans as reality changes.

docs.title: Why
docs.text:
A static plan is not enough. Real execution requires:

  • resource-aware scheduling
  • continuous evidence checks
  • delegation to humans when AI capability is insufficient
  • iterative re-planning based on new data and outcomes

This proposal extends plan generation into plan execution, with orchestration, governance, and adaptive control.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text:
• Plans often assume ideal conditions and static timelines.
  • AI agents can execute some tasks, but not all (legal, physical, negotiation).
  • Human resources are limited and unevenly available.
  • Without re-planning, early deviations compound into failure.

docs.title: Proposed Solution
docs.text:
Build an Execution Engine that:

  1. Converts plan JSON into an executable task graph.
  2. Assigns tasks to AI agents or humans based on capability and risk.
  3. Tracks progress, evidence, and constraints in real time.
  4. Re-plans dynamically as facts change.
  5. Produces audit-ready reports and governance checkpoints.

docs.title: Execution Architecture
docs.text:
PlanExe JSON
  -> Task Graph Builder
  -> Orchestrator
     -> Agent Pool (AI)
     -> Human Queue
     -> Evidence Validator
     -> Risk Gates
  -> Progress + Metrics
  -> Adaptive Re-Plan

docs.title: 1) Task Graph Builder
docs.text:
• Parse PlanExe outputs into a DAG of tasks with:
  • dependencies
  • required inputs/outputs
  • estimated duration and cost
  • risk class and verification level

docs.title: 2) Capability Registry
docs.text:
• Agents and humans register their capabilities as schemas.
  • Each task specifies the capability required.
  • Orchestrator performs matching based on skills, availability, and cost.

docs.title: 3) Orchestrator
docs.text:
• Dispatches tasks to agents or humans.
  • Manages retries, fallbacks, and escalation.
  • Tracks state transitions (queued, running, blocked, done).

docs.title: 4) Human Task Queue
docs.text:
• Dedicated UI showing:
  • task description
  • required evidence
  • urgency and deadline
  • instructions and context
  • Human actions logged with rationale.

docs.title: 5) Evidence Validator
docs.text:
• Every task output must pass a schema and evidence check.
  • Outputs are tagged with confidence.
  • Low-confidence or high-impact results trigger human review.

docs.title: 6) Risk Gates
docs.text:
• High-impact tasks require approval before execution.
  • Risk gates enforce compliance, safety, and budget limits.

docs.title: Capability Matching
docs.text:
• Match task requirements to agent or human skill profiles.
  • Consider risk class, data sensitivity, and cost.

docs.title: Decision Rules
docs.text:
• AI-first for low-risk, repeatable tasks.
  • Human-first for legal, regulatory, and negotiation tasks.
  • Hybrid for tasks requiring AI prep plus human judgment.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text:
• If AI confidence falls below threshold, reroute to human.
  • If humans reject, re-plan or adjust scope.

docs.title: Adaptive Re-Planning
docs.text:
Execution is not linear. The system must re-plan when:

  • task outputs contradict assumptions
  • dependencies are delayed
  • budgets exceed thresholds
  • external factors change (regulation, market, supplier)

docs.title: Re-Planning Loop
docs.text:
1. Detect deviation vs baseline.
  2. Update plan assumptions and constraints.
  3. Recompute schedule and resource allocation.
  4. Issue new tasks or adjust milestones.

docs.title: Re-Plan Outputs
docs.text:
• Updated Gantt (baseline vs current)
  • Revised risk register
  • Decision log with rationale

docs.title: Resource and Capacity Management
docs.text:
• Track available AI resources and human availability.
  • Enforce concurrency limits.
  • Prioritize high-impact work when capacity is constrained.

docs.title: Capacity Profile Example
docs.text:
{
  "ai_agents": 12,
  "human_fte": {
    "legal": 1,
    "engineering": 3,
    "procurement": 0.5
  },
  "max_parallel_tasks": 20
}

docs.title: Task Schema
docs.text:
{
  "task_id": "t_001",
  "title": "Obtain environmental permit",
  "capability": "regulatory_filing",
  "priority": "high",
  "risk_class": "critical",
  "inputs": ["site_plan", "impact_assessment"],
  "outputs": ["permit_document"],
  "status": "queued"
}

docs.title: Execution Event Log
docs.text:
{
  "event_id": "e_445",
  "task_id": "t_001",
  "actor": "human:legal_01",
  "action": "approved",
  "timestamp": "2026-02-11T10:22:00Z",
  "notes": "Permit submitted"
}

docs.title: Reporting Outputs
docs.text:
• Progress dashboard: status by milestone.
  • Risk dashboard: unresolved risks and blockers.
  • Evidence coverage: which claims are verified.
  • Final execution report:
  • timeline vs baseline
  • deviations and corrective actions
  • decision log
  • confidence profile

docs.title: Example Execution Timeline (With Change Events)
docs.text:
Baseline (Day 0):

  • Week 1-2: feasibility + regulatory scoping
  • Week 3-4: procurement + vendor selection
  • Week 5-8: build + integration
  • Week 9: compliance review
  • Week 10: launch

Execution Events:

  • Day 9: Regulator requests additional environmental study.
  • Day 10: Task graph updated, compliance lane becomes critical.
  • Day 11: Human legal capacity bottleneck detected.
  • Day 12: Orchestrator proposes contractor augmentation and re-baselines timeline.
  • Day 14: Updated plan published with new dependencies and budget deltas.

Output: Updated Gantt, risk register, and decision log entry that explains the change and its impact.

docs.title: Failure Handling and Rollback
docs.text: Autonomous execution must assume failure cases and encode recovery:

docs.title: Failure Types
docs.text:
• Data failure: missing or invalid inputs.
  • Task failure: agent returns low-confidence or incorrect output.
  • External failure: third-party dependency delays or rejects.
  • Policy failure: execution violates budget, compliance, or ethics constraints.

docs.title: Recovery Actions
docs.text:
• Retry with alternative agent (for data and task failures).
  • Escalate to human for high-risk or repeated failures.
  • Re-plan to adjust dependencies and milestones.
  • Rollback to last stable snapshot if downstream impacts are unsafe.

docs.title: Rollback Mechanism
docs.text:
• Every milestone creates a snapshot of plan state and evidence.
  • Rollback reverts to the last validated snapshot.
  • All downstream tasks are invalidated and re-queued with updated inputs.

docs.title: AI-to-Human Handoff Contract (SLA + Requirements)
docs.text: For tasks that require human intervention, define a strict handoff contract:

docs.title: Required Fields
docs.text:
• Task purpose and required outcome.
  • Evidence needed and acceptance criteria.
  • Deadline and urgency.
  • Context pack (inputs, dependencies, prior decisions).
  • Suggested next steps and contact references.

docs.title: SLA Targets
docs.text:
• Acknowledgement: within 24 hours.
  • Completion: within task-defined window (default 5 business days).
  • Escalation: if no response within SLA, route to alternate human or reduce scope.

docs.title: Audit Requirement
docs.text:
Every handoff must record:

  • human assignee
  • response timestamp
  • decision rationale
  • evidence provided

docs.title: Safety and Governance
docs.text:
• Immutable audit log for all actions and outputs.
  • Explicit sign-offs on high-impact tasks.
  • Budget and compliance thresholds enforced by policy.
  • Kill-switch to halt execution in emergencies.

docs.title: Success Metrics
docs.text:
• % tasks executed without manual intervention
  • Median deviation vs baseline schedule
  • % high-risk tasks with evidence verified
  • Time saved vs manual execution coordination

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• Over-automation may hide human context.
  • Insufficient human capacity causes bottlenecks.
  • False confidence from AI outputs.

docs.title: Feasibility Tiers
docs.text:
Autonomous execution is feasible only with clear boundaries. Define tiers by risk and controllability:

  • Tier 1 (Feasible Now): low-risk, repeatable tasks (data gathering, summarization, formatting, internal reporting).
  • Tier 2 (Partially Feasible): tasks that can be AI-driven but require human approval (procurement drafts, compliance checklists, vendor shortlists).
  • Tier 3 (Not Feasible Without Human Lead): legal filings, negotiations, physical execution, regulatory approvals, and high-stakes financial commitments.

docs.title: Feasibility Adjustments
docs.text:
To make autonomous execution practical, enforce the following adjustments:

  • Scope limits: constrain execution to well-defined domains and task types.
  • Hard policy gates: require explicit human approval for high-impact steps.
  • Evidence sufficiency checks: block execution when inputs are unverified.
  • Capacity-aware scheduling: align execution with real human availability.
  • Rollback readiness: snapshot after each milestone and auto-revert on critical deviation.

docs.title: Phase 1: Autonomous Coordination
docs.text:
• Task graphing, scheduling, and progress tracking.
  • Human execution for all tasks.
  • Evidence collection and audit logging only.

docs.title: Phase 2: Low-Risk Autonomous Execution
docs.text:
• AI executes low-risk tasks under strict validation.
  • Human approval for all medium and high risk tasks.
  • Adaptive re-planning enabled.

docs.title: Phase 3: Selective Autonomous Execution
docs.text:
• AI executes a broader set of tasks with confidence thresholds.
  • High-risk steps remain human-led.
  • Continuous monitoring and rollback enforced.

docs.title: Scope Checklist (Tier Classification)
docs.text: Use this checklist to classify each task into Tier 1/2/3. If any Tier 3 condition is true, the task is human-led.

docs.title: Tier 1 (AI-Executable)
docs.text:
• Internal analysis or data aggregation only.
  • No external commitments or legal exposure.
  • Outputs are reversible and low-cost to redo.
  • Evidence inputs are verified and structured.

docs.title: Tier 2 (AI-With-Approval)
docs.text:
• External communication or procurement drafts.
  • Moderate budget impact but reversible.
  • Requires validation by a human reviewer.
  • Evidence is strong but not fully audited.

docs.title: Tier 3 (Human-Led)
docs.text:
• Legal filings, regulatory submissions, or compliance sign-offs.
  • Negotiations, contracts, or financial commitments.
  • Physical execution or safety-critical actions.
  • High budget impact or irreversible decisions.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
1. MVP orchestrator with basic DAG execution.
  2. Capability registry and task schema.
  3. Human task queue integration.
  4. Evidence validator and risk gate layer.
  5. Adaptive re-planning and reporting.
  6. Pilot on a real plan with human oversight.

docs.title: Phase A — Execution Core (3–4 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Build canonical execution graph model in code:
  2. task node
  3. dependency edge
  4. execution state machine

  5. risk class and verification requirements

  6. Implement orchestrator service with deterministic scheduling:

  7. queueing
  8. dependency resolution
  9. retries

  10. timeout + cancellation semantics

  11. Add durable event stream:

  12. all state transitions append-only
  13. idempotent event handlers

docs.title: Phase B — Capability and Assignment Layer (2–3 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Define capability contract schema for agents/humans:
  2. capability id
  3. confidence profile
  4. availability window

  5. trust tier

  6. Implement assignment policy engine:

  7. AI-first for low-risk tasks
  8. human-first for legal/regulatory/irreversible actions

  9. hybrid path for AI-prep + human decision

  10. Add capacity-aware scoring:

  11. minimize queue aging
  12. avoid overloading constrained human roles

docs.title: Phase C — Validation + Governance (2 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Add evidence validator gate before task completion.
  2. Add policy gate before high-impact task execution.
  3. Implement emergency kill-switch + rollback to last stable milestone snapshot.

docs.title: Phase D — Adaptive Re-Planning (2–3 weeks)
docs.text:
1. Detect deviation triggers:
  2. cost/schedule threshold breach
  3. failed dependencies

  4. assumption drift from external signals

  5. Re-plan workflow:

  6. clone active graph state
  7. regenerate local schedule around affected subgraph

  8. preserve completed task evidence and audit lineage

  9. Publish delta artifacts:

  10. baseline vs current timeline
  11. budget delta
  12. decision rationale log

docs.title: Data model additions
docs.text:
• execution_tasks
  • execution_events
  • assignment_decisions
  • policy_gate_results
  • replan_snapshots

All tables should be indexed by plan_id, run_id, and task_id for audit and replay.

docs.title: API additions (suggested)
docs.text:
• POST /api/execution/start/{plan_id}
  • GET /api/execution/status/{run_id}
  • POST /api/execution/replan/{run_id}
  • POST /api/execution/stop/{run_id}

docs.title: Rollout safety controls
docs.text:
• Start with “coordination-only mode” (humans execute all tasks)
  • Enable autonomous execution for Tier 1 tasks only
  • Require opt-in per workspace for Tier 2
  • Keep Tier 3 permanently human-led unless explicit governance override

docs.title: Validation checklist
docs.text:
• Event consistency under retries/restarts
  • Correct dependency execution ordering
  • No policy-gated task executes without approval
  • Rollback replay determinism
  • Human handoff SLA conformance

docs.title: Evidence Traceability Ledger: Technical Documentation
docs.text: Author: PlanExe Team Date: 2026-02-11 Status: Proposal Audience: Developers, Auditors, Investors

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Evidence Traceability Ledger is a core component of the PlanExe verification engine. It transforms vague claims into verifiable facts by maintaining a cryptographically-linked ledger of every claim made in a plan and its corresponding evidence.
